title: Quiz: Basis Sets in Quantum Chemistry
description: Test your understanding of basis set concepts and selection

questions.question: Why are Gaussian-type orbitals (GTOs) preferred over Slater-type orbitals (STOs) in practice?

options.text: GTOs have the correct cusp at the nucleus

questions.explanation: The product of two Gaussians centered on different atoms is another Gaussian, allowing all integrals to be evaluated analytically. STOs don't have this property and require expensive numerical integration.

questions.question: What does 'STO-3G' mean?

options.text: 3 Gaussians contracted to approximate each STO

questions.explanation: STO-3G means each Slater-type orbital is approximated by a contraction of 3 Gaussian functions. The contraction coefficients are optimized to best fit the STO shape.

questions.question: What are polarization functions?

options.text: Higher angular momentum functions that describe electron density distortion

questions.explanation: Polarization functions are higher angular momentum functions (e.g., p on hydrogen, d on carbon) that allow the electron density to 'polarize' or distort in response to bonding.

questions.question: When are diffuse functions essential?

options.text: For anions, excited states, and weak interactions

questions.explanation: Diffuse functions (with small exponents) extend far from the nucleus and are essential for anions (loosely bound electrons), Rydberg excited states, and weak interactions like van der Waals forces.

questions.question: A triple-zeta (TZ) basis set uses 3 functions to represent each valence orbital.
questions.explanation: True. In a triple-zeta basis, each valence atomic orbital is represented by 3 basis functions with different exponents, allowing more flexibility in describing the orbital shape.

questions.question: How many qubits are needed to represent H₂O in an STO-3G basis (7 spatial orbitals) using Jordan-Wigner mapping?
questions.explanation: Each spatial orbital corresponds to 2 spin-orbitals (α and β spin). With 7 spatial orbitals, we need 7 × 2 = 14 qubits in Jordan-Wigner mapping.

questions.question: What is the main disadvantage of using larger basis sets?
questions.explanation: The number of two-electron integrals scales as O(N⁴) with basis size N. For correlated methods, the scaling is even worse (O(N⁵) to O(N⁷)), making large basis sets very expensive.

questions.question: Which statements about basis set convergence are true? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Energies converge to the complete basis set (CBS) limit
Double-zeta is always sufficient for accurate thermochemistry
Extrapolation schemes can estimate CBS limit

questions.explanation: a) True: As basis sets grow, energies approach CBS limit. b) True: Geometries are less sensitive to basis size than energies. c) False: Triple-zeta or larger is typically needed for accurate thermochemistry. d) True: Extrapolation from TZ/QZ results can estimate CBS energies.
